content:
Let \(R=2008\) and \(\alpha=\sqrt R\).  One shows by direct “kissing‐circle” algebra (or by Descartes’ theorem) that the radii \(r_n\) satisfy
\[
\sqrt{r_n}
\;=\;
\frac{\alpha}{\,\alpha-(n-1)\,}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
r_n=\frac{R}{\bigl(\alpha-(n-1)\bigr)^2}\,.
\]
In order to remain externally tangent to the big circle of radius \(R\), we need \(r_n<R\).  Hence
\[
\frac{R}{\bigl(\alpha-(n-1)\bigr)^2}<R
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\alpha-(n-1)>1
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
n<\alpha=\sqrt{2008}\approx44.82.
\]
Thus the largest integer \(n\) with \(n<\sqrt{2008}\) is \(n=44\).  Answer:
\[
\boxed{44}.
\]

In order to remain externally tangent to the big circle of radius \(R\), we need \(r_n<R\).  Hence
\[
\frac{R}{\bigl(\alpha-(n-1)\bigr)^2}<R
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\alpha-(n-1)>1
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
